text: This video demonstrates robotic low anterior resection with total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral subpingo oophorectomy, and then blocked pelvic peritonectomy followed by HIPEC for the multimodality treatment of recto sigmoid cancer with peritoneal seating. A seventy-eight-year-old woman experienced a change in the bowel habits and bloody stool for two months. Colonoscopy showed an ulcerative mass in the rectosigmoid colon, fifteen centimeters above the anal verge. MRI revealed rectosigmoid colon cancer with suspected serosal invasion and multiple pericardic lymphadenopathy. The patient underwent robotic low anterior resection with total hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-orphyrectomy, and pelvic peritonectomy.

The surgeon begins dissection from the visceral peritoneum, revealing the right external iliac vessels. The round ligament and ovarian vessels are visible crossing the external iliac vessels. The right round ligament is clipped and cut, exposing the right ureter and gonadal vessels. The gonadal vessels are identified, mobilized, clipped, and cut. Dissection continues towards the pelvic floor, preserving the hypogastric nerve, along the vascular plane between the mesorectum and presacral fascia. The procedure is mirrored on the left side, with division of the left gonadal vessels and transection of the left round ligament.

The surgeon identifies and mobilizes the left ureter to prevent injury. The left gonadal vessels are clipped and cut. The surgical team then moves to the anterior aspect of the uterus to develop the bladder flap by tenting and incising the vesicouterine peritoneum. The urinary bladder is pushed down and moved off the lower segment of the uterus, revealing the uterine artery. The right uterine artery is clipped and divided.

The surgeon clips and divides the left uterine artery, then makes an incision around the cervix. The vagina is opened and circumferentially cut, freeing it from surrounding tissues. The rectum is retracted and the mesorectum is divided using monopolar cautery. After complete division, the rectum is divided with an endogia stapler. The vaginal cuff is closed with two OV lock sutures using a robotic system, and a stable anastomosis is performed under laparoscopic vision.

Following the completion of multivisceral resection and peritonectomy, the HIPEC equipment is set up using robotic surgery ports. Four catheters are placed: two outflow catheters in the pelvic cavity, one inflow catheter in the left subphrenic area, and one inflow catheter in the subhepatic area. Rubber drains are connected to the inflow tubing, and outflow conduits are connected to the outflow tubing, all linked to the perfusion machine.

We used two thermometers to monitor the temperature of the fluid flowing in and out of the process. The patient was subjected to HIPEC for one hour using the infusate containing Folfax, Fi-FU, one thousand five hundred milligrams per square meter, Leucovarin, one hundred seventy milligrams per square meter, and Oxaliplatin, eighty five milligrams per square meter, with a temperature controlled at forty two degrees Celsius. We adopted closed technology in our organization. Compared with open technology, closed technology can prevent heat loss and reduce exposure to chemotherapy drugs. Surgical pathology examination revealed rectal sigmoid colon cancer with metastasis and original lymph node and peritoneal seeding. The consult time was three hours and ten minutes. The duration of post-operative hospital stay was forty days due to post-operative ileus and pneumonia. Both these conditions improved after administration of supportive treatment. The patient was followed up for forty months and good tumor control was noted.
